title: I let Claude Code take my job (it did better than me)
summary: Alberta Tech conducted an experiment comparing Claude Code against their own coding job performance. The creator tests whether Claude Code can outperform human developers and explores integration with Higgsfield's model context protocol tooling.
writeup: A software engineer and short-form comedy creator runs a controlled experiment: can Claude Code, orchestrated via Higgsfield's MCP server, automate the production of her own style of satirical tech videos using ByteDance's CogVideoX (C Dance 2.0) model? The answer is mostly yes, with the telling caveat that an AI-generated version of her existing script outperformed a real video featuring actual creators on Instagram trial reels. The result raises a sharper question about what actually drives video performance, which turns out to be the script and concept rather than authentic production.

key_points: Claude Code acts as orchestrator rather than generator: it calls the Higgsfield MCP server to access C Dance 2.0 (ByteDance's video model), splits a 20-to-30-second script into roughly 5-second chunks, uploads the creator's reference images and audio separately, and generates all clips in parallel before assembling them into a folder for manual editing in CapCut.

Passing images and audio as separate inputs, rather than a full reference video, was the critical fix for getting coherent lip-sync output; the creator reports no technical explanation for why the full-video input failed, describing video generation prompting as an art rather than a science.

The hope core aesthetic (heavy filter, music, frequent B-roll cutaways) was deliberately chosen because it masks AI generation artifacts; B-roll was sourced from Pexels rather than generated, keeping costs and usage conservative.

The AI-generated video posted to Instagram trial reels reached 6,000 views, 228 likes, and 83 shares in 11 hours, with no viewer comments identifying it as AI; a comparable real video with actual creators had roughly 100 likes, suggesting the script drives performance more than authenticity of production.

The one artifact viewers could have caught, a mispronounced 'Schrödinger,' went unnoticed, which the creator takes as evidence that audiences are not yet reliably detecting AI video at this quality level.

The creator ends with discomfort about undisclosed AI content rather than a production verdict: she would consider this workflow only with explicit disclosure, and the experiment has made her more skeptical of every video she encounters going forward.
